serving.description: 1 dry serving (45 g uncooked, yields approximately 125 g cooked)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of uncooked quinoa is approximately 45 g (about 3 tablespoons dry), which expands to roughly 125 g of cooked quinoa after absorbing water during cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Quinoa, uncooked — USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 168917 (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for quinoa, uncooked (FDC ID 168917) used as primary nutrient reference. Singapore HPB database values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are consistent with USDA values and have been cross-validated. USDA values used for saturated fat, sugar, and additional micronutrients not reported in HPB.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 53 for quinoa (uncooked basis) sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 64.16 × 0.45) / 100 = 53 × 28.87 / 100 ≈ 15.3. Serving size used: 45 g uncooked (28.87 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100 g) with high potassium (563 mg/100 g) and magnesium (197 mg/100 g) supporting vasodilation and blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Quinoa is an excellent grain choice for hypertensive individuals due to its negligible sodium content, high potassium-to-sodium ratio (~113:1), and substantial magnesium content; cook without added salt and rinse thoroughly before cooking to remove saponins.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 53) moderated by high protein (14.1 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (7 g/100 g) which slow gastric emptying and attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite its high dry-weight carbohydrate content (64.2 g/100 g uncooked), quinoa's low GI of 53 and high protein and fibre content make it suitable for type 2 diabetics when portion-controlled based on cooked weight (100 g uncooked yields approximately 280 g cooked with ~21 g carbohydrate per 100 g cooked).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable lipid profile with low saturated fat (0.71 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high polyunsaturated fat (3.29 g/100 g), and high dietary fibre supporting LDL reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Quinoa supports cardiovascular health through its low saturated fat content, absence of cholesterol, high dietary fibre (7 g/100 g) which aids LDL cholesterol reduction, and high magnesium and potassium content which support endothelial function and blood pressure control.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome immune modulation; complete protein profile for tissue repair; anti-inflammatory polyphenol content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Quinoa is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients as a complete protein whole grain with anti-inflammatory properties; rinse thoroughly before cooking to remove saponins that may cause gut irritation in sensitive individuals, and combine with omega-3 sources to maximise anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Quinoa (uncooked) refers to the raw, dried seeds of Chenopodium quinoa, a flowering plant native to the Andean region of South America, particularly Peru and Bolivia, where it has been cultivated for over 5,000 years as a staple crop. The uncooked seeds are small, round, and typically beige or ivory in colour (though red and black varieties also exist), with a firm texture and a mild, slightly nutty flavour. Before cooking, quinoa must be rinsed thoroughly under cold water to remove its natural saponin coating, which imparts a bitter taste. Quinoa is cooked by simmering in water or broth at a 1:2 ratio (1 part quinoa to 2 parts liquid) for approximately 15 minutes until the seeds become translucent and the characteristic white germ ring separates. 100 g of uncooked quinoa yields approximately 280 g of cooked quinoa. Nutritionally, uncooked quinoa is exceptionally dense: it provides 368 kcal, 14.1 g of complete protein (containing all nine essential amino acids including lysine, which is rare in plant foods), 6.1 g of fat (predominantly polyunsaturated), 64.2 g of carbohydrate, and 7 g of dietary fibre per 100 g. It is rich in magnesium (197 mg), phosphorus (457 mg), potassium (563 mg), iron (4.57 mg), and folate (184 mcg DFE). Its glycemic index is low at approximately 53, making it suitable for blood glucose management. Quinoa is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal, making it widely suitable across dietary requirements. It is used globally as a rice or couscous substitute, in salads, grain bowls, soups, and as a breakfast porridge base. Clinically, it is recommended for type 2 diabetics, hypertensive patients, and those managing cardiovascular risk due to its low GI, low sodium, high potassium, and favourable lipid profile.
common_user_queries: How much protein does quinoa have?
What is the glycemic index of quinoa?
What is the difference between cooked and uncooked quinoa nutrition?
Is quinoa good for weight loss?
Can I eat quinoa if I have rheumatoid arthritis?
What are the benefits of quinoa for heart health?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168917) for quinoa uncooked, an exact match. HPB Singapore database values for core macros and selected micronutrients are consistent with USDA values and have been cross-validated. GI value of 53 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). All values are well-established in multiple approved sources.
notes: Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168917) — quinoa is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol. Trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for uncooked quinoa. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as quinoa is a plant food with no B12. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. All per_100g values are on an uncooked/dry-weight basis. Cooked quinoa (per 100 g) will have approximately one-third of these nutrient values due to water absorption during cooking (100 g uncooked yields approximately 280 g cooked). Saponin content: quinoa should be rinsed thoroughly before cooking to remove bitter saponins. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 (quinoa seed) is a defensible exact match; FoodEx2 code A00LC (Quinoa) is sourced from the EFSA FoodEx2 classification system; SNOMED CT code 412061001 (Quinoa substance) is a defensible match. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, sugar_g, and all vitamin/mineral values beyond those in HPB are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 168917 as HPB does not report these fields for this entry.
